title: Brunei

infobox.value: * [Brunei Malay](/wiki/Brunei_Malay "Brunei Malay") * [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") * [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") ([Malaysian Tamil](/wiki/Malaysian_Tamil "Malaysian Tamil")) * [Tutong](/wiki/Tutong_language "Tutong language") * [Kedayan](/wiki/Kedayan "Kedayan") * [Belait](/wiki/Belait_language "Belait language") * [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") ([Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien"), [Hakka Chinese](/wiki/Hakka_Chinese "Hakka Chinese"), [Standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese") and [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese")) * [Murut](/wiki/Lun_Bawang_language "Lun Bawang language") * [Dusun](/wiki/Dusun_language "Dusun language") * [Brunei Bisaya](/wiki/Brunei_Bisaya_language "Brunei Bisaya language")

* 80.7% [Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") ([official](/wiki/State_religion "State religion")) * 10.7% [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Brunei "Christianity in Brunei") * 7.8% [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Brunei "Buddhism in Brunei") * 0.8% other (includes [Indigenous beliefs](/wiki/List_of_ethnic_religions#Asia "List of ethnic religions"))

**Brunei**,[[b]](#cite_note-20) officially **Brunei Darussalam**,[[c]](#cite_note-24) is a country in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), situated on the northern coast of the island of [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"). Apart from its coastline on the [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea"), it is completely surrounded by the [Malaysian state](/wiki/States_and_federal_territories_of_Malaysia "States and federal territories of Malaysia") of [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak"), with its territory [bifurcated](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bifurcated "wiktionary:bifurcated") by the Sarawak district of [Limbang](/wiki/Limbang_District "Limbang District"). Brunei is the only sovereign state entirely on Borneo; the remainder of the island is divided between its multi-landmass neighbours of [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") and [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"). As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit), the country had a population of 466,330,[[22]](#cite_note-depd.gov.bn-25) of whom approximately 64,409 resided in the capital and largest city of [Bandar Seri Begawan](/wiki/Bandar_Seri_Begawan "Bandar Seri Begawan"). Its official language is [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"), and [Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") is the [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion") of the country, although [other religions are nominally tolerated](/wiki/Religion_in_Brunei "Religion in Brunei"). The [government of Brunei](/wiki/Government_of_Brunei "Government of Brunei") is an [absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy") ruled by the [Sultan](/wiki/Sultan_of_Brunei "Sultan of Brunei"), and it implements a fusion of [English common law](/wiki/English_law#Common_law "English law") and jurisprudence inspired by Islam, including [sharia](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia").

At the [Sultanate of Brunei](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Brunei "Sultanate of Brunei")'s peak during the reign of Sultan [Bolkiah](/wiki/Bolkiah "Bolkiah") (1485–1528), the state is claimed to have had control over the most of Borneo, including modern-day [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") and [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah"), as well as the [Sulu archipelago](/wiki/Sulu_archipelago "Sulu archipelago") and the islands off the northwestern tip of Borneo. There are also claims to its historical control over Seludong, in which Southeast Asian scholars believe the name of the location in question is actually in reference to Mount Selurong, in Indonesia,[[23]](#cite_note-Abinales2005-26) or Serudong River in eastern Sabah.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESaunders200242-27) The maritime state of Brunei was visited by the surviving crew of the [Magellan Expedition](/wiki/Timeline_of_Magellan%27s_circumnavigation "Timeline of Magellan's circumnavigation") in 1521, and in 1578 it fought against Spain in the [Castilian War](/wiki/Castilian_War "Castilian War").

During the 19th century, the Bruneian Empire began to decline. The Sultanate ceded [Kuching](/wiki/Kuching "Kuching") to [James Brooke](/wiki/James_Brooke "James Brooke") and installed him as the [White Rajah](/wiki/White_Rajah "White Rajah"), and it ceded Sabah to the British [North Borneo Chartered Company](/wiki/North_Borneo_Chartered_Company "North Borneo Chartered Company"). In 1888, Brunei became a [British protectorate](/wiki/British_protectorate "British protectorate") and was assigned a [British resident](/wiki/Resident_(title) "Resident (title)") as colonial manager in 1906. After the [Japanese occupation](/wiki/Japanese_colonial_empire "Japanese colonial empire") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), a new constitution was written in 1959. In 1962, a small [armed rebellion](/wiki/Brunei_revolt "Brunei revolt") against the monarchy which was indirectly related to the [Indonesia–Malaysia confrontation](/wiki/Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation "Indonesia–Malaysia confrontation") was ended with British assistance and led to the ban of the pro-independent [Brunei People's Party](/wiki/Brunei_People%27s_Party "Brunei People's Party"). The revolt had also influenced the Sultan's decision not to join the Malaysian Federation while [it was being formed](/wiki/Malaysia_Agreement "Malaysia Agreement"). Britain's protectorate over Brunei would eventually end on 1 January 1984, becoming a fully [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state").

Brunei has been led by Sultan [Hassanal Bolkiah](/wiki/Hassanal_Bolkiah "Hassanal Bolkiah") since 1967, and the country's [unicameral legislature](/wiki/Unicameral_legislature "Unicameral legislature"), the [Legislative Council](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Brunei "Legislative Council of Brunei"), is simply consultative and are all appointed by the Sultan. The country's wealth derives from its extensive petroleum and natural gas fields. Economic growth during the 1990s and 2000s has transformed Brunei into an industrialised country, with its GDP increasing 56% between 1999 and 2008. [Political stability](/wiki/Politics_of_Brunei#Political_conditions "Politics of Brunei") is maintained by the [House of Bolkiah](/wiki/House_of_Bolkiah "House of Bolkiah") by providing a [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state") for its citizens, with free or significant subsidies in regards to housing, healthcare and education. It ranks "very high" on the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") (HDI)—the second-highest among Southeast Asian states after [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), with which it maintains close relations including a [Currency Interchangeability Agreement](/wiki/Brunei%E2%80%93Singapore_Currency_Interchangeability_Agreement "Brunei–Singapore Currency Interchangeability Agreement"). According to the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") (IMF), Brunei is [ranked seventh in the world by gross domestic product per capita at purchasing power parity](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (PPP) per capita"). Brunei is a member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"), the [East Asia Summit](/wiki/East_Asia_Summit "East Asia Summit"), the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation"), the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement"), the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), and [ASEAN of the World Trade Forum.](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN")

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

According to local historiography, Brunei was founded by Awang Alak Betatar, later to be [Sultan Muhammad Shah](/wiki/Muhammad_Shah_of_Brunei "Muhammad Shah of Brunei"), reigning around AD 1400. He moved from Garang in the [Temburong](/wiki/Temburong "Temburong") District[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200926-28) to the [Brunei River](/wiki/Brunei_River "Brunei River") estuary, discovering Brunei. According to legend, upon landing he exclaimed, "*Baru nah!*" (loosely translated as "that's it!" or "there!"), from which the name "Brunei" was derived.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Vienne201627-29) He was the first Muslim ruler of Brunei.[[27]](#cite_note-30) Before the rise of the [Bruneian Empire](/wiki/Bruneian_Empire "Bruneian Empire") under the Muslim Bolkiah dynasty, Brunei is believed to have been under Buddhist rulers.[[28]](#cite_note-Nicholl1980-31) It was renamed *Barunai* in the 14th century, possibly influenced by the [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") **varuṇ** (वरुण), meaning "seafarers".[[29]](#cite_note-Minahan2012-32) The word "[Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo")" is of the same origin.

In the country's full name of **Negara Brunei Darussalam**, the word **darussalam** ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): دار السلام) means "Abode of Peace", while **negara** means "country" in [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"). A shortened version of the official Malay name, "Brunei Darussalam", has also entered common usage, particularly in official contexts, and is present in the [United Nations Group of Experts on Geographical Names](/wiki/United_Nations_Group_of_Experts_on_Geographical_Names "United Nations Group of Experts on Geographical Names") geographical database,[[30]](#cite_note-33) as well as official ASEAN[[31]](#cite_note-34) and Commonwealth[[32]](#cite_note-35) listings.

The earliest Western mention of Brunei is from the [Bolognese](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna") traveller [Ludovico di Varthema](/wiki/Ludovico_di_Varthema "Ludovico di Varthema") in 1550:

> We arrived at the island of Bornei *(Brunei or Borneo)*, which is distant from the *[Maluch](/wiki/Maluku_Islands "Maluku Islands")* about two hundred miles [three hundred kilometres], and we found that it was somewhat larger than the aforesaid and much lower. The people are pagans and are men of goodwill. Their colour is whiter than that of the other sort ... in this island justice is well administered ...[[33]](#cite_note-36)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Brunei](/wiki/History_of_Brunei "History of Brunei")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/The_Tomb_of_the_King_of_Boni_%28Brunei%29_Nanjing.JPG/250px-The_Tomb_of_the_King_of_Boni_%28Brunei%29_Nanjing.JPG)](/wiki/File:The_Tomb_of_the_King_of_Boni_(Brunei)_Nanjing.JPG)

The [Tomb of the King of Boni](/wiki/Tomb_of_the_King_of_Boni "Tomb of the King of Boni") in [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing"), Jiangsu, China

Areas comprising what is now Brunei participated in the [Maritime Jade Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road"), as ascertained by archaeological research. The trading network existed for 3,000 years, between 2000 BC to 1000 AD.[[34]](#cite_note-37)[[35]](#cite_note-38)[[36]](#cite_note-39)[[37]](#cite_note-40) The settlement known as Vijayapura was a vassal-state to the Buddhist [Srivijaya](/wiki/Srivijaya "Srivijaya") empire and was thought to be located in Borneo's Northwest which flourished in the 7th Century.[[38]](#cite_note-41) Vijayapura itself upon earlier in its history, was a rump state of the fallen multi-ethnic: Austronesian, Austroasiatic and Indian, [Funan Civilization](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Funan "Kingdom of Funan"); previously located in what is now Cambodia.[[39]](#cite_note-Nicholl-42): 36 This alternative Srivijaya known as Vijayapura referring to Brunei, was known to Arabic sources as "Sribuza".[[40]](#cite_note-43)

One of the earliest Chinese records of an independent kingdom in Borneo is the 977 AD letter to the Chinese emperor from the ruler of Boni, which some scholars believe to refer to Borneo.[[41]](#cite_note-Hutton2000-44) The Bruneians regained their independence from Srivijaya due to the onset of a Javanese-Sumatran war.[[42]](#cite_note-45) In 1225, the Chinese official [Zhao Rukuo](/wiki/Zhao_Rukuo "Zhao Rukuo") reported that Boni had 100 warships to protect its trade, and that there was great wealth in the kingdom.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200943-46) Marco Polo suggested in his memoirs that the Great Khan or the ruler of the Mongol Empire, attempted and failed many times in invading "Great Java" which was the European name for Bruneian controlled Borneo.[[44]](#cite_note-47)[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

According to Wang Zhenping, in the 1300s, the *Yuan Dade nanhai zhi* or "Yuan dynasty Dade period southern sea records" reported that Brunei conquered or administered [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") and [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") as well as the Philippine kingdoms of [Butuan](/wiki/Rajahnate_of_Butuan "Rajahnate of Butuan"), [Sulu](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Sulu "Sultanate of Sulu"), [Ma-i](/wiki/Ma-i "Ma-i") (Mindoro), Malilu 麻裏蘆 ([Manila](/wiki/Maynila_(historical_polity) "Maynila (historical polity)")), [Shahuchong](/wiki/Sanmalan "Sanmalan") 沙胡重 (Siocon or [Zamboanga](/wiki/Zamboanga_City "Zamboanga City")), Yachen 啞陳 [Oton](/wiki/Oton "Oton"), and 文杜陵 Wenduling ([Mindanao](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Maguindanao "Sultanate of Maguindanao")),[[45]](#cite_note-48) which would regain their independence at a later date.[[46]](#cite_note-49)

In the 14th century, the [Javanese](/wiki/Java "Java") manuscript *[Nagarakretagama](/wiki/Nagarakretagama "Nagarakretagama")*, written by [Prapanca](/wiki/Mpu_Prapanca "Mpu Prapanca") in 1365, mentioned *Barune* as the constituent state of Hindu [Majapahit](/wiki/Majapahit "Majapahit"),[[47]](#cite_note-50) which had to make an annual [tribute](/wiki/Tribute "Tribute") of 40 [katis](/wiki/Catty "Catty") of [camphor](/wiki/Camphor "Camphor"). In 1369, Sulu which was also formerly part of Majapahit, had successfully rebelled and then attacked Boni, and had invaded the Northeast Coast of Borneo[[48]](#cite_note-51) and afterwards had looted the capital of its treasure and gold including sacking two sacred pearls. A fleet from Majapahit succeeded in driving away the Sulus, but Boni was left weaker after the attack.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200944-52) A Chinese report from 1371 described Boni as poor and totally controlled by Majapahit.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200945-53) When the Chinese admiral [Zheng He](/wiki/Zheng_He "Zheng He") visited the Brunei in the early 15th century, he founded a major trading port which included Chinese people who were actively trading with China.[[51]](#cite_note-54)

During the 15th century, Boni had seceded from Majapahit and then converted to Islam. Thus transforming into the independent Sultanate of Brunei. Brunei became a [Hashemite](/wiki/Hashemite "Hashemite") state when she allowed the Arab Emir of Mecca, [Sharif Ali](/wiki/Sharif_Ali "Sharif Ali"), to become her third sultan.

As customary for close affiliation and alliances in Southeast Asia, the royal family of Luzon intermarried with the ruling houses of the [Sultanate of Brunei](/wiki/Bruneian_Empire "Bruneian Empire").[[52]](#cite_note-Junker1998-55) Intermarriage was a common strategy for Southeast Asian states to extend their influence.[[53]](#cite_note-Scott1994-56) However, Islamic Brunei's power was not uncontested in Borneo since it had a Hindu rival in a state founded by Indians called [Kutai](/wiki/Kutai "Kutai") in the south which they overpowered but never destroyed.

Nevertheless, by the 16th century, Islam was firmly rooted in Brunei, and the country had built one of its biggest mosques. In 1578, Alonso Beltrán, a Spanish traveller, described it as being five stories tall and built on the water.[[54]](#cite_note-57)

### War with Spain and decline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: War with Spain and decline")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Brunei_territorial_lose_%281400%E2%80%931890%29.gif/250px-Brunei_territorial_lose_%281400%E2%80%931890%29.gif)](/wiki/File:Brunei_territorial_lose_(1400%E2%80%931890).gif)

Brunei territorial losses from 1400 to 1890

Brunei briefly rose to prominence in Southeast Asia when the Portuguese occupied [Malacca](/wiki/Malacca "Malacca") and thereby forced the wealthy and powerful but displaced Muslim refugees there to relocate to nearby Sultanates such as Brunei. The Bruneian Sultan then intervened in a territorial conflict between [Hindu Tondo](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Tondo "Kingdom of Tondo") and [Muslim Manila](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Maynila "Kingdom of Maynila") in the Philippines by appointing the Bruneian descended [Rajah Ache](/wiki/Rajah_Matanda "Rajah Matanda") of Manila as admiral of the Bruneian navy in a rivalry against Tondo and as the enforcer of Bruneian interests in the Philippines. He subsequently encountered the Magellan expedition[[55]](#cite_note-58) wherein [Antonio Pigafetta](/wiki/Antonio_Pigafetta "Antonio Pigafetta") noted that under orders from his grandfather the Sultan of Brunei, Ache had previously sacked the Buddhist city of Loue in Southwest Borneo for being faithful to the old religion and rebelling against the authority of Sultanate.[[56]](#cite_note-59) However, [European influence](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism") gradually brought an end to Brunei's [regional power](/wiki/Regional_power "Regional power"), as Brunei entered a period of decline compounded by internal strife over royal succession. In the face of these invasions by European Christian powers, the [Ottoman Caliphate](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") aided the beleaguered Southeast Asian Sultanates by making [Aceh](/wiki/Ottoman_expedition_to_Aceh "Ottoman expedition to Aceh") a [protectorate](/wiki/Protectorate "Protectorate") and sending expeditions to reinforce, train and equip the local [mujahideen](/wiki/Mujahideen "Mujahideen").[[57]](#cite_note-60) Turks were routinely migrating to Brunei as evidenced by the complaints of Manila Oidor Melchor Davalos who in his 1585 report, say that Turks were coming to Sumatra, Borneo and Ternate every year, including defeated veterans from the [Battle of Lepanto](/wiki/Battle_of_Lepanto "Battle of Lepanto").[[58]](#cite_note-61)

Spain [declared war](/wiki/Castille_War "Castille War") in 1578, planning to attack and capture [Kota Batu](/wiki/Kota_Batu,_Brunei-Muara "Kota Batu, Brunei-Muara"), Brunei's capital at the time. This was based in part on the assistance of two Bruneian [noblemen](/wiki/Pengiran#Brunei "Pengiran"), Pengiran Seri Lela and Pengiran Seri Ratna. The former had travelled to [Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila"), then the centre of the Spanish colony. Manila itself was captured from Brunei, Christianised and made a territory of the [Viceroyalty of New Spain](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_New_Spain "Viceroyalty of New Spain") which was centered in Mexico City. Pengiran Seri Lela came to offer Brunei as a [tributary](/wiki/Tributary_state "Tributary state") to Spain for help to recover the throne usurped by his brother, Saiful Rijal.[[59]](#cite_note-62) The Spanish agreed that if they succeeded in conquering Brunei, Pengiran Seri Lela would be appointed as the sultan, while Pengiran Seri Ratna would be the new [Bendahara](/wiki/Bendahara "Bendahara").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/%E4%B8%87%E5%9B%BD%E6%9D%A5%E6%9C%9D%E5%9B%BE_Brunei_%28%E6%B1%B6%E8%8E%B1%E5%9C%8B%29_delegates_in_Peking_in_1761.jpg/250px-%E4%B8%87%E5%9B%BD%E6%9D%A5%E6%9C%9D%E5%9B%BE_Brunei_%28%E6%B1%B6%E8%8E%B1%E5%9C%8B%29_delegates_in_Peking_in_1761.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E4%B8%87%E5%9B%BD%E6%9D%A5%E6%9C%9D%E5%9B%BE_Brunei_(%E6%B1%B6%E8%8E%B1%E5%9C%8B)_delegates_in_Peking_in_1761.jpg)

Chinese depiction of delegates from Brunei arriving in Beijing. From the *[Ten Thousand Nations Coming to Pay Tribute](/wiki/Ten_Thousand_Nations_Coming_to_Pay_Tribute "Ten Thousand Nations Coming to Pay Tribute")*, 1761.

In March 1578, a fresh Spanish fleet had arrived from Mexico and settled at the Philippines. They were led by [De Sande](/wiki/Francisco_de_Sande "Francisco de Sande"), acting as [Capitán-General](/wiki/Capit%C3%A1n-General "Capitán-General"). He organised an expedition from Manila for Brunei, consisting of 400 Spaniards and Mexicans, 1,500 [Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_people "Filipino people") natives, and 300 Borneans.[[60]](#cite_note-63) The campaign was one of many, which also included action in [Mindanao](/wiki/Mindanao "Mindanao") and [Sulu](/wiki/Sulu_Archipelago "Sulu Archipelago").[[61]](#cite_note-64)[[62]](#cite_note-65) The racial make-up of the Christian side was diverse since it were usually made up of [Mestizos](/wiki/Mestizos "Mestizos"), [Mulattoes](/wiki/Mulattoes "Mulattoes") and Amerindians ([Aztecs](/wiki/Aztecs "Aztecs"), [Mayans](/wiki/Maya_peoples "Maya peoples") and [Incans](/wiki/Incans "Incans")) who were gathered and sent from Mexico and were led by Spanish officers who had worked together with native Filipinos in military campaigns across the Southeast Asia.[[63]](#cite_note-66) The Muslim side was also equally racially diverse. In addition to the native Malay warriors, the Ottomans had repeatedly sent military expeditions to nearby [Aceh](/wiki/Ottoman_expedition_to_Aceh "Ottoman expedition to Aceh"). The expeditions were composed mainly of [Turks](/wiki/Turkic_people "Turkic people"), [Egyptians](/wiki/Egyptians "Egyptians"), [Swahilis](/wiki/Swahili_people "Swahili people"), [Somalis](/wiki/Somalis "Somalis"), [Sindhis](/wiki/Sindhis "Sindhis"), [Gujaratis](/wiki/Gujaratis "Gujaratis") and [Malabars](/wiki/Malabars "Malabars").[[64]](#cite_note-67) These expeditionary forces had also spread to other nearby Sultanates such as Brunei and had taught new fighting tactics and techniques on how to forge cannons.[[65]](#cite_note-68)

Eventually, the Spanish captured the capital on 16 April 1578, with the help of Pengiran Seri Lela and Pengiran Seri Ratna. The Sultan Saiful Rijal and Paduka Seri Begawan Sultan Abdul Kahar were forced to flee to Meragang then to [Jerudong](/wiki/Jerudong "Jerudong"). In Jerudong, they made plans to chase the conquering army away from Brunei. Suffering high fatalities due to a [cholera](/wiki/Cholera "Cholera") or [dysentery](/wiki/Dysentery "Dysentery") outbreak,[[66]](#cite_note-69)[[67]](#cite_note-70) the Spanish decided to abandon Brunei and returned to Manila on 26 June 1578, after 72 days.[[68]](#cite_note-71)

Pengiran Seri Lela died in August or September 1578, probably from the same illness suffered by his Spanish allies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] There was suspicion that the legitimist sultan could have been poisoned by the ruling sultan.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Seri Lela's daughter, a Bruneian princess, "Putri", had left with the Spanish, she abandoned her claim to the crown and then she married a Christian [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_people "Tagalog people"), named Agustín de Legazpi de Tondo.[[69]](#cite_note-72) [Agustin de Legaspi](/wiki/Agustin_de_Legaspi "Agustin de Legaspi") along with his family and associates were soon implicated in the [Conspiracy of the Maharlikas](/wiki/Conspiracy_of_the_Maharlikas "Conspiracy of the Maharlikas"), an attempt by Filipinos to link up with the Brunei Sultanate and Japanese Shogunate to expel the Spaniards from the Philippines.[[70]](#cite_note-:1-73) However, upon the Spanish suppression of the conspiracy, the Bruneian descended aristocracy of precolonial Manila were exiled to [Guerrero, Mexico](/wiki/Guerrero,_Mexico "Guerrero, Mexico") which consequently later became a center of the [Mexican war of independence against Spain](/wiki/Isidoro_Montes_de_Oca "Isidoro Montes de Oca").[[71]](#cite_note-enchistoria-74)[[72]](#cite_note-riquezahis-75)

The local Brunei accounts[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200941-76) of the Castilian War differ greatly from the generally accepted view of events. What was called the Castilian War was seen as a heroic episode, with the Spaniards being driven out by [Bendahara Sakam](/wiki/Bendahara_Sakam "Bendahara Sakam"), purportedly a brother of the ruling sultan, and a thousand native warriors. Most historians consider this to be a [folk-hero](/wiki/Folk-hero "Folk-hero") account, which probably developed decades or centuries after.[[74]](#cite_note-77)

Brunei eventually descended into anarchy. The country suffered a [civil war](/wiki/Bruneian_Civil_War_of_1660 "Bruneian Civil War of 1660") from 1660 to 1673.

### British intervention

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: British intervention")]

See also: [British Brunei Administrators](/wiki/British_Brunei_Administrators "British Brunei Administrators"), [British Borneo](/wiki/British_Borneo "British Borneo"), [Malcolm Stewart Hannibal McArthur](/wiki/Malcolm_Stewart_Hannibal_McArthur "Malcolm Stewart Hannibal McArthur"), [Protectorate Agreement of Brunei (1888)](/wiki/Protectorate_Agreement_of_Brunei_(1888) "Protectorate Agreement of Brunei (1888)"), and [Report On Brunei](/wiki/Report_On_Brunei "Report On Brunei")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/The_signing_of_the_Treaty_of_Labuan_on_18_December_1846.jpg/250px-The_signing_of_the_Treaty_of_Labuan_on_18_December_1846.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_signing_of_the_Treaty_of_Labuan_on_18_December_1846.jpg)

British adventurer [James Brooke](/wiki/James_Brooke "James Brooke") negotiating with the [Sultan of Brunei](/wiki/Sultan_of_Brunei "Sultan of Brunei"), which led to the signing of the [Treaty of Labuan](/wiki/Treaty_of_Labuan "Treaty of Labuan"), 1846

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Control_of_the_island_of_Borneo.png/250px-Control_of_the_island_of_Borneo.png)](/wiki/File:Control_of_the_island_of_Borneo.png)

Boundaries of Brunei (green) since 1890

The British have intervened in the affairs of Brunei on several occasions. Britain attacked Brunei in July 1846 due to internal conflicts over who was the rightful Sultan.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200952-78)

In the 1880s, the decline of the [Bruneian Empire](/wiki/Bruneian_Empire "Bruneian Empire") continued. The sultan granted land (now Sarawak) to [James Brooke](/wiki/James_Brooke "James Brooke"), who had helped him quell a rebellion, and allowed him to establish the [Raj of Sarawak](/wiki/Raj_of_Sarawak "Raj of Sarawak"). Over time, Brooke and his nephews (who succeeded him) leased or annexed more land. Brunei lost much of its territory to him and his dynasty, known as the [White Rajahs](/wiki/White_Rajahs "White Rajahs").

Sultan [Hashim Jalilul Alam Aqamaddin](/wiki/Hashim_Jalilul_Alam_Aqamaddin "Hashim Jalilul Alam Aqamaddin") appealed to the British to stop further encroachment by the Brookes.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam20093-79) The [Protectorate Agreement](/wiki/Protectorate_Agreement_of_Brunei_(1888) "Protectorate Agreement of Brunei (1888)") was negotiated by [Sir Hugh Low](/wiki/Sir_Hugh_Low "Sir Hugh Low") and signed into effect on 17 September 1888. The treaty said that the sultan "could not cede or lease any territory to foreign powers without British consent"; it provided Britain effective control over Brunei's external affairs, making it a [British protected state](/wiki/British_protected_state "British protected state") (which continued until 1984).[[77]](#cite_note-cia-80)[[78]](#cite_note-81) But, when the Raj of Sarawak annexed Brunei's [Pandaruan District](/wiki/Limbang_Division "Limbang Division") in 1890,[[79]](#cite_note-82) the British did not take any action to stop it. They did not regard either Brunei or the Raj of Sarawak as 'foreign' (per the Treaty of Protection). This final annexation by Sarawak left Brunei with its current small land mass and separation into two parts.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200958-83)

The British Foreign Office sent Consul [Malcolm Stewart Hannibal McArthur](/wiki/Malcolm_Stewart_Hannibal_McArthur "Malcolm Stewart Hannibal McArthur") to assess Brunei and make recommendations on future British foreign policy in the region.[[81]](#cite_note-84) McArthur produced his [Report On Brunei in 1904](/wiki/Report_On_Brunei "Report On Brunei") where he recommended the implementation of the [Malayan British Residency system](/wiki/Federated_Malay_States "Federated Malay States") and the continued protection of Brunei.[[82]](#cite_note-85) [British residents](/wiki/British_resident "British resident") were introduced in Brunei under the Supplementary Protectorate Agreement in 1906.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200959-86)[[84]](#cite_note-87) The residents were to advise the sultan on all matters of administration. Over time, the resident assumed more executive control than the sultan. The residential system ended in 1959.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200967-88)

### Discovery of oil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Discovery of oil")]

Petroleum was discovered in 1929 after several fruitless attempts.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200912-89) Two men, F. F. Marriot and T. G. Cochrane, smelled oil near the [Seria](/wiki/Seria "Seria") river in late 1926.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200913-90) They informed a [geophysicist](/wiki/Geophysicist "Geophysicist"), who conducted a survey there. In 1927, gas seepages were reported in the area. Seria Well Number One (S-1) was drilled on 12 July 1928. Oil was struck at 297 metres (974 ft) on 5 April 1929. Seria Well Number 2 was drilled on 19 August 1929, and, as of 2009[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit), continues to produce oil.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200914-91) Oil production was increased considerably in the 1930s with the development of more oil fields. In 1940, oil production was at more than six million barrels.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200914-91) The British Malayan Petroleum Company (now [Brunei Shell Petroleum](/wiki/Brunei_Shell_Petroleum "Brunei Shell Petroleum") Company) was formed on 22 July 1922.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200915-92) The first offshore well was drilled in 1957.[[90]](#cite_note-93) Oil and natural gas have been the basis of Brunei's development and wealth since the late 20th century.

### Japanese occupation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Japanese occupation")]

Main article: [Japanese occupation of British Borneo](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_British_Borneo "Japanese occupation of British Borneo")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Sultan_Ahmad_Tajuddin_%28AWM_P10841.001%29.JPG/250px-Sultan_Ahmad_Tajuddin_%28AWM_P10841.001%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sultan_Ahmad_Tajuddin_(AWM_P10841.001).JPG)

[Ahmad Tajuddin](/wiki/Ahmad_Tajuddin "Ahmad Tajuddin"), the [27th Sultan of Brunei](/wiki/List_of_Sultans_of_Brunei "List of Sultans of Brunei"), with members of his court in April 1941, eight months before the Japanese invaded Brunei

The Japanese invaded Brunei on 16 December 1941, eight days after their [attack on Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor") on the United States Navy.[[91]](#cite_note-:0-94) They landed 10,000 troops of the [Kawaguchi Detachment](/wiki/Kawaguchi_Detachment "Kawaguchi Detachment") from [Cam Ranh Bay](/wiki/Cam_Ranh_Bay "Cam Ranh Bay") at [Kuala Belait](/wiki/Kuala_Belait "Kuala Belait"). After six days' fighting, they occupied the entire country. The only Allied troops in the area were the 2nd Battalion of the [15th Punjab Regiment](/wiki/15th_Punjab_Regiment "15th Punjab Regiment") based at [Kuching](/wiki/Kuching "Kuching"), Sarawak.[[92]](#cite_note-95)

Once the Japanese occupied Brunei, they made an agreement with [Sultan Ahmad Tajuddin](/wiki/Ahmad_Tajuddin "Ahmad Tajuddin") over governing the country. Inche Ibrahim (known later as Pehin Datu Perdana Menteri Dato Laila Utama Awang Haji Ibrahim), a former Secretary to the British Resident, Ernest Edgar Pengilly, was appointed [chief administrative officer](/wiki/Chief_administrative_officer "Chief administrative officer") under the Japanese Governor. The Japanese had proposed that Pengilly retain his position under their administration, but he declined. Both he and other British nationals still in Brunei were interned by the Japanese at [Batu Lintang camp](/wiki/Batu_Lintang_camp "Batu Lintang camp") in Sarawak. While the British officials were under Japanese guard, Ibrahim made a point of personally shaking each one by the hand and wishing him well.[[93]](#cite_note-96)[[94]](#cite_note-97)

The Sultan retained his throne and was given a pension and honours by the Japanese. During the later part of the occupation, he resided at Tantuya, [Limbang](/wiki/Limbang "Limbang") and had little to do with the Japanese. Most of the Malay government officers were retained by the Japanese. Brunei's administration was reorganised into five prefectures, which included British [North Borneo](/wiki/North_Borneo "North Borneo"). The Prefectures included [Baram](/wiki/Miri_Division "Miri Division"), Labuan, [Lawas](/wiki/Lawas "Lawas"), and [Limbang](/wiki/Limbang "Limbang"). Ibrahim hid numerous significant government documents from the Japanese during the occupation. Pengiran Yusuf (later YAM Pengiran Setia Negara Pengiran [Haji Mohd Yusuf](/wiki/Haji_Mohd_Yusuf "Haji Mohd Yusuf")), along with other Bruneians, was sent to Japan for training. Although in the area the day of the [atomic bombing of Hiroshima](/wiki/Atomic_bombings_of_Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki "Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki"), Yusuf survived.[[95]](#cite_note-98)

The British had anticipated a Japanese attack, but lacked the resources to defend the area because of their engagement in the war in Europe. The troops from the Punjab Regiment filled in the [Seria](/wiki/Seria "Seria") oilfield oilwells with concrete in September 1941 to deny the Japanese their use. The remaining equipment and installations were destroyed when the [Japanese invaded Malaya](/wiki/Japanese_invasion_of_Malaya "Japanese invasion of Malaya"). By the end of the war, 16 wells at [Miri](/wiki/Miri,_Malaysia "Miri, Malaysia") and Seria had been restarted, with production reaching about half the pre-war level. Coal production at [Muara](/wiki/Muara,_Brunei "Muara, Brunei") was also recommenced, but with little success.[[91]](#cite_note-:0-94)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Japanese_Battleship_Nagato_1944.jpg/250px-Japanese_Battleship_Nagato_1944.jpg)](/wiki/File:Japanese_Battleship_Nagato_1944.jpg)

*[Nagato](/wiki/Japanese_battleship_Nagato "Japanese battleship Nagato")*, *[Tone](/wiki/Japanese_cruiser_Tone_(1937) "Japanese cruiser Tone (1937)")*, *[Yamato](/wiki/Japanese_battleship_Yamato "Japanese battleship Yamato")* and *[Musashi](/wiki/Japanese_battleship_Musashi "Japanese battleship Musashi")* in Brunei Bay in October 1944

During the occupation, the Japanese had their language taught in schools, and Government officers were required to learn Japanese. The local currency was replaced by what was to become known as [*duit pisang* (banana money)](/wiki/Banana_money "Banana money"). From 1943 hyper-inflation destroyed the currency's value and, at the end of the war, this currency was worthless. Allied attacks on shipping eventually caused trade to cease. Food and medicine fell into short supply, and the population suffered from famine and disease.[[91]](#cite_note-:0-94)

The [airport](/wiki/Brunei_International_Airport "Brunei International Airport") runway was constructed by the Japanese during the occupation, and in 1943 Japanese naval units were based in [Brunei Bay](/wiki/Brunei_Bay "Brunei Bay") and Labuan. The naval base was destroyed by Allied bombing, but the airport runway survived. The facility was developed as a public airport. In 1944 the Allies began a bombing campaign against the occupying Japanese, which destroyed much of the town and Kuala Belait, but missed [Kampong Ayer](/wiki/Kampong_Ayer "Kampong Ayer").[[96]](#cite_note-99)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Japanese_Surrender%2C_Labuan_%28AWM_115989%29.JPG/250px-Japanese_Surrender%2C_Labuan_%28AWM_115989%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Japanese_Surrender,_Labuan_(AWM_115989).JPG)

[Major-General Wootten](/wiki/George_Wootten "George Wootten") of the Australian 9th Division with [Lieutenant-General Masao Baba](/wiki/Masao_Baba "Masao Baba") (signing) of the Japanese 37th Division at the surrender ceremony at Labuan on 10 September 1945

On 10 June 1945, the [Australian 9th Division](/wiki/Australian_9th_Division "Australian 9th Division") landed at Muara under [Operation Oboe Six](/wiki/Battle_of_North_Borneo "Battle of North Borneo") to recapture Borneo from the Japanese. They were supported by American air and naval units. Brunei town was bombed extensively and recaptured after three days of heavy fighting. Many buildings were destroyed, including the Mosque. The Japanese forces in Brunei, Borneo, and Sarawak, under Lieutenant-General Masao Baba, formally surrendered at Labuan on 10 September 1945. The British Military Administration took over from the Japanese and remained until July 1946.[[97]](#cite_note-100)

### Post-World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Post-World War II")]

After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), a new government was formed in Brunei under the [British Military Administration](/wiki/British_Military_Administration_(Borneo) "British Military Administration (Borneo)") (BMA). It consisted mainly of Australian officers and servicemen.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200979-101) The administration of Brunei was passed to the Civil Administration on 6 July 1945. The Brunei State Council was also revived that year.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200980-102) The BMA was tasked to revive the Bruneian economy, which was extensively damaged by the Japanese during their occupation. They also had to put out the fires on the wells of Seria, which had been set by the Japanese prior to their defeat.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200980-102)

Before 1941, the Governor of the Straits Settlements, based in Singapore, was responsible for the duties of British High Commissioner for Brunei, [Sarawak](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sarawak "Kingdom of Sarawak"), and [North Borneo](/wiki/North_Borneo "North Borneo") (now [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah")).[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200981-103) The first [British High Commissioner](/wiki/British_High_Commissioner "British High Commissioner") for Brunei was the Governor of Sarawak, Sir Charles Ardon Clarke. The [Barisan Pemuda](/wiki/Barisan_Pemuda "Barisan Pemuda") ("Youth Front"; abbreviated as BARIP) was the first political party to be formed in Brunei, on 12 April 1946. The party intended to "preserve the sovereignty of the Sultan and the country, and to defend the rights of the [Malays](/wiki/Ethnic_Malays "Ethnic Malays")".[[101]](#cite_note-History131-104) BARIP also contributed to the composition of the country's national anthem. The party was dissolved in 1948 due to inactivity.

In 1959, a new constitution was written declaring Brunei a self-governing state, while its foreign affairs, security, and defence remained the responsibility of the United Kingdom.[[102]](#cite_note-US_DOS-105) A small [rebellion](/wiki/Rebellion "Rebellion") erupted against the monarchy in 1962, which was suppressed with help of the UK.[[103]](#cite_note-106) Known as the [Brunei Revolt](/wiki/Brunei_Revolt "Brunei Revolt"), the rebellion contributed to the Sultan's decision to opt out of joining the emerging state now called Malaysia under the umbrella of [North Borneo Federation](/wiki/North_Borneo_Federation "North Borneo Federation").[[102]](#cite_note-US_DOS-105)

Brunei gained its independence from the United Kingdom on 1 January 1984.[[102]](#cite_note-US_DOS-105) The official National Day, which celebrates the country's independence, is held by tradition on 23 February.[[104]](#cite_note-107)

### Writing of the Constitution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Writing of the Constitution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Omar_Ali_Saifuddien_III.jpg/250px-Omar_Ali_Saifuddien_III.jpg)](/wiki/File:Omar_Ali_Saifuddien_III.jpg)

Sultan [Omar Ali Saifuddien III](/wiki/Omar_Ali_Saifuddien_III "Omar Ali Saifuddien III")

In July 1953, Sultan [Omar Ali Saifuddien III](/wiki/Omar_Ali_Saifuddien_III "Omar Ali Saifuddien III") formed a seven-member committee named *Tujuh Serangkai*, to determine the citizens' views regarding a written constitution for Brunei. In May 1954, the Sultan, Resident and High Commissioner met to discuss the findings of the committee. They agreed to authorise the drafting of a constitution. In March 1959, Sultan Omar Ali Saifuddien III led a delegation to London to discuss the proposed Constitution.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200998-108) The British delegation was led by Sir [Alan Lennox-Boyd](/wiki/Alan_Lennox-Boyd "Alan Lennox-Boyd"), Secretary of State for the Colonies. The British Government later accepted the draft constitution.

On 29 September 1959, the Constitution Agreement was signed in [Brunei Town](/wiki/Bandar_Seri_Begawan "Bandar Seri Begawan"). The agreement was signed by Sultan Omar Ali Saifuddien III and Sir Robert Scott, the Commissioner-General for Southeast Asia.
It included the following provisions:[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200959-86)

* The Sultan was made the Supreme Head of State.
* Brunei was responsible for its internal administration.
* The British Government was responsible for foreign and defence affairs only.
* The post of Resident was abolished and replaced by a British High Commissioner.

Five councils were established:[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam2009100-109)

* The Executive Council
* [The Legislative Council of Brunei](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Brunei "Legislative Council of Brunei")
* The Privy Council
* The Council of Succession
* The State Religious Council

### National development plans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: National development plans")]

A series of National Development Plans was initiated by the 28th Sultan of Brunei, [Omar Ali Saifuddien III](/wiki/Omar_Ali_Saifuddien_III "Omar Ali Saifuddien III").

The first was introduced in 1953.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200992-110) A total sum of B$100 million was approved by the Brunei State Council for the plan. E.R. Bevington, from the Colonial Office in [Fiji](/wiki/Fiji "Fiji"), was appointed to implement it.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam2009129-111) A US$14 million Gas Plant was built under the plan. In 1954, survey and exploration work were undertaken by the Brunei Shell Petroleum on both offshore and onshore fields. By 1956, production reached 114,700 [bpd](/wiki/Barrels_per_day "Barrels per day").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/THE_BRITISH_ARMY_IN_BRUNEI%2C_JANUARY_1963.jpg/250px-THE_BRITISH_ARMY_IN_BRUNEI%2C_JANUARY_1963.jpg)](/wiki/File:THE_BRITISH_ARMY_IN_BRUNEI,_JANUARY_1963.jpg)

British soldiers in the [British protectorate](/wiki/British_protectorate "British protectorate") of Brunei on guard in the [Seria oilfield](/wiki/Seria_Field "Seria Field"), January 1963

The plan also aided the development of public education. By 1958, expenditure on education totalled at $4 million.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam2009129-111) Communications were improved, as new roads were built and reconstruction at [Berakas Airport](/wiki/Old_Airport,_Brunei "Old Airport, Brunei") was completed in 1954.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200921-112)

The second National Development Plan was launched in 1962.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200921-112) A major oil and gas field was discovered in 1963. Developments in the oil and gas sector have continued, and oil production has steadily increased since then.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200996-113) The plan also promoted the production of meat and eggs for consumption by citizens. The fishing industry increased its output by 25% throughout the course of the plan. The [deepwater port](/wiki/List_of_Panamax_ports "List of Panamax ports") at [Muara](/wiki/Muara,_Brunei "Muara, Brunei") was also constructed during this period. Power requirements were met, and studies were made to provide electricity to rural areas.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHistory_for_Brunei_Darussalam200996-113) Efforts were made to eradicate [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria"), an endemic disease in the region, with the help of the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization"). Malaria cases were reduced from 300 cases in 1953 to only 66 cases in 1959.[[111]](#cite_note-History129-114) The death rate was reduced from 20 per thousand in 1947 to 11.3 per thousand in 1953.[[111]](#cite_note-History129-114) Infectious disease has been prevented by public sanitation and improvement of drainage, and the provision of piped pure water to the population.[[111]](#cite_note-History129-114)

### Independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Independence")]

On 14 November 1971, Sultan [Hassanal Bolkiah](/wiki/Hassanal_Bolkiah "Hassanal Bolkiah") left for London to discuss matters regarding the amendments to the 1959 constitution. A new agreement was signed on 23 November 1971 with the British representative being [Anthony Royle](/wiki/Anthony_Royle,_Baron_Fanshawe_of_Richmond "Anthony Royle, Baron Fanshawe of Richmond").[[112]](#cite_note-115)

Under this agreement, the following terms were agreed upon:

* Brunei was granted full internal self-government
* The UK would still be responsible for external affairs and defence.
* Brunei and the UK agreed to share the responsibility for security and defence.

This agreement also caused [Gurkha units](/wiki/British_Military_Garrison_Brunei "British Military Garrison Brunei") to be deployed in Brunei, where they remain up to this day.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Sultan_of_Brunei_with_Janin_Erih.jpg/250px-Sultan_of_Brunei_with_Janin_Erih.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sultan_of_Brunei_with_Janin_Erih.jpg)

Sultan [Hassanal Bolkiah](/wiki/Hassanal_Bolkiah "Hassanal Bolkiah") (right), handing his credentials to ambassador [Janin Erih](/wiki/Janin_Erih "Janin Erih") in 2004

On 7 January 1979, another treaty was signed between Brunei and the United Kingdom. It was signed with Lord Goronwy-Roberts being the representative of the UK. This agreement granted Brunei to take over international responsibilities as an independent nation. Britain agreed to assist Brunei in diplomatic matters. In May 1983, it was announced by the UK that the date of independence of Brunei would be 1 January 1984.[[113]](#cite_note-116)

On 31 December 1983, a mass gathering was held on main mosques on all four of the districts of the country and at midnight, on 1 January 1984, the Proclamation of Independence was read by Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah. The sultan subsequently assumed the title "[His Majesty](/wiki/Majesty "Majesty")", rather than the previous "[His Royal Highness](/wiki/His_Royal_Highness "His Royal Highness")".[[114]](#cite_note-117) Brunei was [admitted to the United Nations](/wiki/Member_states_of_the_United_Nations "Member states of the United Nations") on 22 September 1984, becoming the organisation's 159th member.[[115]](#cite_note-118)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In October 2013, Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah announced his intention to impose [Penal Code](/wiki/Penal_Code "Penal Code") from [Sharia](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") on the country's Muslims, which make up roughly two-thirds of the country's population.[[116]](#cite_note-119) This would be implemented in three phases, culminating in 2016, and making Brunei the first and only country in East Asia to introduce Sharia into its penal code, excluding the subnational Indonesian special territory of [Aceh](/wiki/Aceh "Aceh").[[117]](#cite_note-120) The move attracted international criticism,[[118]](#cite_note-MSNBC_2014-05-07-121) the United Nations expressing "deep concern".[[119]](#cite_note-BBC-27216798-122)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Brunei](/wiki/Geography_of_Brunei "Geography of Brunei")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Brunei_location_map_Topographic.png/330px-Brunei_location_map_Topographic.png)](/wiki/File:Brunei_location_map_Topographic.png)

A topographic and geographic limits map of Brunei

Brunei is a [Southeast Asian](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") country consisting of two unconnected parts with a total area of 5,765 square kilometres (2,226 sq mi) on the island of [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"). It has 161 kilometres (100 mi) of coastline next to the [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea"), and it shares a 381 km (237 mi) [border with Malaysia](/wiki/Brunei%E2%80%93Malaysia_border "Brunei–Malaysia border"). It has 500 square kilometres (193 sq mi) of territorial waters, and a 200-nautical-mile (370 km; 230 mi) [exclusive economic zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone "Exclusive economic zone").[[77]](#cite_note-cia-80)

About 97% of the population lives in the larger western part ([Belait](/wiki/Belait_District "Belait District"), [Tutong](/wiki/Tutong_District "Tutong District"), and [Brunei-Muara](/wiki/Brunei_and_Muara "Brunei and Muara")), while only about 10,000 people live in the mountainous eastern part ([Temburong District](/wiki/Temburong_District "Temburong District")). The total population of Brunei is approximately 408,000 as of July 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit), of which around 150,000 live in the capital [Bandar Seri Begawan](/wiki/Bandar_Seri_Begawan "Bandar Seri Begawan").[[120]](#cite_note-123) Other major towns are the port town of [Muara](/wiki/Muara,_Brunei "Muara, Brunei"), the oil-producing town of [Seria](/wiki/Seria "Seria") and its neighbouring town, [Kuala Belait](/wiki/Kuala_Belait "Kuala Belait"). In Belait District, the [Panaga](/wiki/Panaga "Panaga") area is home to large numbers of Europeans expatriates, due to [Royal Dutch Shell](/wiki/Royal_Dutch_Shell "Royal Dutch Shell") and British Army housing, and several recreational facilities are located there.[[121]](#cite_note-124)

Most of Brunei is within the [Borneo lowland rain forests](/wiki/Borneo_lowland_rain_forests "Borneo lowland rain forests") ecoregion, which covers most of the island. Areas of [mountain rain forests](/wiki/Borneo_montane_rain_forests "Borneo montane rain forests") are located inland.[[122]](#cite_note-125) In Brunei [forest cover](/wiki/Forest_cover "Forest cover") is around 72% of the total land area, equivalent to 380,000 hectares (ha) of forest in 2020, down from 413,000 hectares (ha) in 1990. In 2020, naturally regenerating forest covered 374,740 hectares (ha) and planted forest covered 5,260 hectares (ha). Of the naturally regenerating forest 69% was reported to be [primary forest](/wiki/Primary_forest "Primary forest") (consisting of native tree species with no clearly visible indications of human activity) and around 5% of the forest area was found within protected areas. For the year 2015, 100% of the forest area was reported to be under [public ownership](/wiki/State_ownership "State ownership").[[123]](#cite_note-126)[[124]](#cite_note-127)

The [climate of Brunei](/wiki/Climate_of_Brunei "Climate of Brunei") is [tropical](/wiki/Tropical_climate "Tropical climate") equatorial that is a [tropical rainforest climate](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest_climate "Tropical rainforest climate")[[77]](#cite_note-cia-80) more subject to the [Intertropical Convergence Zone](/wiki/Intertropical_Convergence_Zone "Intertropical Convergence Zone") than the [trade winds](/wiki/Trade_wind "Trade wind") and with no or rare [cyclones](/wiki/Cyclone "Cyclone"). Brunei is exposed to the risks stemming from climate change along with other [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN") member states.[[125]](#cite_note-128)[[126]](#cite_note-129)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

Main articles: [Politics of Brunei](/wiki/Politics_of_Brunei "Politics of Brunei") and [Government of Brunei](/wiki/Government_of_Brunei "Government of Brunei")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Sultan_Hassanal_Bolkiah_-_53810840215.jpg/250px-Sultan_Hassanal_Bolkiah_-_53810840215.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sultan_Hassanal_Bolkiah_-_53810840215.jpg)

[Hassanal Bolkiah](/wiki/Hassanal_Bolkiah "Hassanal Bolkiah"), [Sultan of Brunei](/wiki/Sultan_of_Brunei "Sultan of Brunei")

Brunei's political system is governed by the constitution and the national tradition of the Malay Islamic Monarchy (*[Melayu Islam Beraja](/wiki/Melayu_Islam_Beraja "Melayu Islam Beraja")*; MIB). The three components of MIB cover Malay culture, Islamic religion, and the political framework under the monarchy.[[127]](#cite_note-bruneipress.com.bn-130) It has a legal system based on [English common law](/wiki/English_common_law "English common law"), although Islamic law (*[sharia](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia")*) supersedes this in some cases.[[77]](#cite_note-cia-80) Brunei [has a parliament](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Brunei "Legislative Council of Brunei"), but there are no elections; instead, the members are appointed by the sultan. The last election was held in [1962](/wiki/1962_Bruneian_district_council_election "1962 Bruneian district council election").[[128]](#cite_note-131)

Under Brunei's 1959 constitution, the Sultan, currently [Hassanal Bolkiah](/wiki/Hassanal_Bolkiah "Hassanal Bolkiah"), is the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") with full executive authority. Following the [Brunei Revolt](/wiki/Brunei_Revolt "Brunei Revolt") of 1962, this authority has included [emergency powers](/wiki/State_of_emergency "State of emergency"), which are renewed every two years, meaning that Brunei has technically been under [martial law](/wiki/Martial_law "Martial law") since then.[[102]](#cite_note-US_DOS-105) Hassanal Bolkiah also serves as the state's prime minister, [finance minister](/wiki/Finance_minister "Finance minister") and [defence minister](/wiki/Defence_minister "Defence minister").[[129]](#cite_note-132)

### Administrative divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Administrative divisions")]

Main article: [Administrative divisions of Brunei](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Brunei "Administrative divisions of Brunei")

Brunei is divided into four [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Brunei "Districts of Brunei") (*daerah*), namely [Brunei-Muara](/wiki/Brunei-Muara_District "Brunei-Muara District"), [Belait](/wiki/Belait_District "Belait District"), [Tutong](/wiki/Tutong_District "Tutong District") and [Temburong](/wiki/Temburong_District "Temburong District"). Brunei-Muara District is the smallest yet the most populous and home to the country's capital, Bandar Seri Begawan. Belait is the birthplace and centre of the country's oil and gas industry. Temburong is an [exclave](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave") and is separated from the rest of the country by the [Brunei Bay](/wiki/Brunei_Bay "Brunei Bay") and Malaysian [state](/wiki/States_of_Malaysia "States of Malaysia") of [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak"). Tutong is home to [Tasek Merimbun](/wiki/Tasek_Merimbun "Tasek Merimbun"), the country's largest natural lake.

Each district is divided into several [mukims](/wiki/Mukims_of_Brunei "Mukims of Brunei"). Altogether, there are 39 mukims in Brunei. Each mukim encompasses several [villages](/wiki/Villages_of_Brunei "Villages of Brunei") (*kampung* or *kampong*).

Bandar Seri Begawan and towns in the country (except [Muara](/wiki/Pekan_Muara "Pekan Muara") and [Bangar](/wiki/Bangar,_Brunei "Bangar, Brunei")) are administered as [Municipal Board areas](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Brunei "Municipalities of Brunei") (*kawasan Lembaga Bandaran*). Each municipal area may constitute villages or mukims, partially or as a whole. Bandar Seri Begawan and a few of the towns also function as the capitals of the districts where they are located.

A district and its constituent mukims and villages are administered by a District Office (*Jabatan Daerah*). Meanwhile, municipal areas are governed by Municipal Departments (*Jabatan Bandaran*). Both District Offices and Municipal Departments are government departments under the [Ministry of Home Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Home_Affairs_(Brunei) "Ministry of Home Affairs (Brunei)").

### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

Main article: [Foreign relations of Brunei](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Brunei "Foreign relations of Brunei")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Pres._Obama_and_HM_Bolkiah_APEC_2015.jpg/250px-Pres._Obama_and_HM_Bolkiah_APEC_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pres._Obama_and_HM_Bolkiah_APEC_2015.jpg)

Brunei's Sultan and Foreign Minister [Hassanal Bolkiah](/wiki/Hassanal_Bolkiah "Hassanal Bolkiah") meets with U.S. President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), 18 November 2015.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Keroncong_Kemayoran_di_Perayaan_50_Tahun_Sultan_Hassanal_Bolkiah_Bertakhta_%282c%29.jpg/250px-Keroncong_Kemayoran_di_Perayaan_50_Tahun_Sultan_Hassanal_Bolkiah_Bertakhta_%282c%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Keroncong_Kemayoran_di_Perayaan_50_Tahun_Sultan_Hassanal_Bolkiah_Bertakhta_(2c).jpg)

Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah with Indonesian President [Joko Widodo](/wiki/Joko_Widodo "Joko Widodo"), 6 October 2017

Until 1979, Brunei's foreign relations were managed by the [UK government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom"). After that, they were handled by the Brunei Diplomatic Service. After independence in 1984, this Service was upgraded to ministerial level and is now known as the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.[[130]](#cite_note-133)

Officially, Brunei's foreign policy is as follows:[[131]](#cite_note-134)

* Mutual respect of others' territorial sovereignty, integrity and independence;
* The maintenance of friendly relations among nations;
* Non-interference in the internal affairs of other countries; and
* The maintenance and the promotion of peace, security and stability in the region.

With its traditional ties with the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), Brunei became the 49th member of the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") immediately on the day of its independence on 1 January 1984.[[132]](#cite_note-135) As one of its first initiatives toward improved regional relations, Brunei joined [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN") on 7 January 1984, becoming the sixth member. To achieve recognition of its sovereignty and independence, it joined the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") as a full member on 21 September of that same year.[[133]](#cite_note-136)

As an [Islamic country](/wiki/Islamic_state "Islamic state"), Brunei became a full member of the Organisation of the Islamic Conference (now the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation")) in January 1984 at the Fourth Islamic Summit held in [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco").[[134]](#cite_note-137)

After its accession to the [Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation](/wiki/Asia-Pacific_Economic_Cooperation "Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation") forum (APEC) in 1989, Brunei hosted the APEC Economic Leaders' Meeting in November 2000 and the ASEAN Regional Forum (ARF) in July 2002.[[135]](#cite_note-APEC_2000-138) Brunei became a founding member of the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") (WTO) on 1 January 1995,[[136]](#cite_note-WTO_2008-139) and is a major player in [BIMP-EAGA](/wiki/BIMP-EAGA "BIMP-EAGA"), which was formed during the Inaugural Ministers' Meeting in [Davao](/wiki/Davao_City "Davao City"), Philippines, on 24 March 1994.[[137]](#cite_note-BIMP_1994-140)

Brunei shares a close relationship with [Singapore](/wiki/Brunei%E2%80%93Singapore_relations "Brunei–Singapore relations") and the [Philippines](/wiki/Brunei%E2%80%93Philippines_relations "Brunei–Philippines relations"). In April 2009, Brunei and the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") signed a [Memorandum of Understanding](/wiki/Memorandum_of_Understanding "Memorandum of Understanding") (MOU) that seeks to strengthen the bilateral cooperation of the two countries in the fields of agriculture and farm-related trade and investments.[[138]](#cite_note-141)

Brunei is one of many nations to lay claim to some of the disputed [Spratly Islands](/wiki/Spratly_Islands "Spratly Islands").[[139]](#cite_note-dispute-142) The status of [Limbang](/wiki/Limbang "Limbang") as part of [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") has been disputed by Brunei since the area was first annexed in 1890.[[139]](#cite_note-dispute-142) The issue was reportedly settled in 2009, with Brunei agreeing to accept the border in exchange for [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") giving up claims to oil fields in Bruneian waters.[[140]](#cite_note-bt-143) The Brunei government denies this and says that their claim on Limbang was never dropped.[[141]](#cite_note-144)[[142]](#cite_note-145)

Brunei was the chair for ASEAN in 2013.[[143]](#cite_note-146) It also hosted the ASEAN summit on that same year.[[144]](#cite_note-RA-147)

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Military")]

Main articles: [Royal Brunei Armed Forces](/wiki/Royal_Brunei_Armed_Forces "Royal Brunei Armed Forces") and [British Military Garrison Brunei](/wiki/British_Military_Garrison_Brunei "British Military Garrison Brunei")

Brunei maintains three infantry battalions stationed around the country.[[102]](#cite_note-US_DOS-105) The [Brunei navy](/wiki/Royal_Brunei_Navy "Royal Brunei Navy") has several "Ijtihad"-class patrol boats purchased from a German manufacturer. The United Kingdom also maintains a base in [Seria](/wiki/Seria "Seria"), the centre of the oil industry in Brunei. A [Gurkha](/wiki/Gurkha "Gurkha") battalion of 1,500 personnel is stationed there.[[102]](#cite_note-US_DOS-105) United Kingdom military personnel are stationed there under a defence agreement signed between the two countries.[[102]](#cite_note-US_DOS-105)

A [Bell 212](/wiki/Bell_212 "Bell 212") operated by the air force crashed in [Kuala Belait](/wiki/Kuala_Belait "Kuala Belait") on 20 July 2012 with the loss of 12 of the 14 crew on board. The cause of the accident has yet to be ascertained.[[145]](#cite_note-148) The crash is the worst aviation incident in the history of Brunei.

The Army is currently acquiring new equipment,[[146]](#cite_note-149) including [UAVs](/wiki/UAV "UAV") and [S-70i Black Hawks](/wiki/Sikorsky_S-70 "Sikorsky S-70").[[147]](#cite_note-150)

Brunei's Legislative Council proposed an increase of the defence budget for the 2016–17 fiscal year of about five per cent to 564 million Brunei dollars ($408 million). This amounts to about ten per cent of the state's total national yearly expenditure and represents around 2.5 per cent of GDP.[[148]](#cite_note-151)

### Legal system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Legal system")]

Brunei has numerous courts in its judicial branch. The highest court, though subject in civil cases to the appellate jurisdiction of the [Judicial Committee of the Privy Council](/wiki/Judicial_Committee_of_the_Privy_Council "Judicial Committee of the Privy Council"),[[149]](#cite_note-152) is the Supreme Court, which consists of the Court of Appeal and High Court. Both of these have a chief justice and two judges.[[77]](#cite_note-cia-80)

#### Religious rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Religious rights")]

Main article: [Freedom of religion in Brunei](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion_in_Brunei "Freedom of religion in Brunei")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Brunei_International_Airport_Surau.jpg/250px-Brunei_International_Airport_Surau.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brunei_International_Airport_Surau.jpg)

Brunei International Airport Mosque

In The Laws of Brunei, the right of non-Muslims to practice their faith is guaranteed by the [1959 Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Brunei "Constitution of Brunei"). However, celebrations and prayers must be confined to places of worship and private residences.[[150]](#cite_note-m.bt.com.bn-153) Upon adopting Sharia Penal Code, the Ministry of Religious Affairs banned Christmas decorations in public places, but did not forbid celebration of Christmas in places of worship and private premises.[[151]](#cite_note-Brunei_unfairly_hurt-154)
On 25 December 2015, 4,000 out of 18,000 estimated local Catholics attended the mass of Christmas Day and Christmas Eve.[[150]](#cite_note-m.bt.com.bn-153) In 2015, the then-[head](/wiki/Cornelius_Sim "Cornelius Sim") of the Catholic Church in Brunei told *[The Brunei Times](/wiki/The_Brunei_Times "The Brunei Times")*, "To be quite honest there has been no change for us this year; no new restrictions have been laid down, although we fully respect and adhere to the existing regulations that our celebrations and worship be [confined] to the compounds of the church and private residences".[[150]](#cite_note-m.bt.com.bn-153)

Brunei's revised penal code came into force in phases, commencing on 22 April 2014 with offences punishable by fines or imprisonment.[[152]](#cite_note-Brunei140430-155)[[153]](#cite_note-DW-140430-156) The complete code, due for final implementation later,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] stipulated the death penalty for numerous offences (both violent and non-violent), such as insult or defamation of [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad"), insulting any verses of the Quran and Hadith, [blasphemy](/wiki/Blasphemy "Blasphemy"), declaring oneself a prophet or non-Muslim, robbery, rape, adultery, sodomy, extramarital sexual relations for Muslims, and murder. Stoning to death was the specified "method of execution for crimes of a sexual nature". Rupert Colville, spokesperson for the [Office of the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights](/wiki/Office_of_the_UN_High_Commissioner_for_Human_Rights "Office of the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights") (OHCHR) declared that, "Application of the death penalty for such a broad range of offences contravenes international law."[[154]](#cite_note-UN_2014-157)

#### Women and children

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Women and children")]

Main article: [Women in Brunei](/wiki/Women_in_Brunei "Women in Brunei")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/US_Navy_111004-N-VP123-071_Lt._j.g._Artemas_Richardson%2C_a_public_affairs_officer_aboard_the_Arleigh_Burke-class_guided-missile_destroyer_USS_Dewey.jpg/250px-US_Navy_111004-N-VP123-071_Lt._j.g._Artemas_Richardson%2C_a_public_affairs_officer_aboard_the_Arleigh_Burke-class_guided-missile_destroyer_USS_Dewey.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_111004-N-VP123-071_Lt._j.g._Artemas_Richardson,_a_public_affairs_officer_aboard_the_Arleigh_Burke-class_guided-missile_destroyer_USS_Dewey.jpg)

Headscarves called *[tudong](/wiki/Tudong "Tudong")* are compulsory for Brunei's Muslim schoolgirls.

A 2010 [U.S. Department of State](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State") report stated that discrimination against women is a problem in Brunei.[[155]](#cite_note-state.gov-158) The law prohibits sexual harassment and stipulates that whoever assaults or uses criminal force, intending thereby to outrage or knowing it is likely to outrage the modesty of a person, shall be punished with imprisonment for as much as five years and [caning](/wiki/Caning "Caning"). The law stipulates imprisonment of up to 30 years, and caning with not fewer than 12 strokes for rape. The law does not criminalise [spousal rape](/wiki/Marital_rape "Marital rape"); it explicitly states that sexual intercourse by a man with his wife, as long as she is not under 13 years of age, is not rape. Protections against sexual assault by a spouse are provided under the amended Islamic Family Law Order 2010 and Married Women Act Order 2010. The penalty for breaching a protection order is a fine not exceeding BN$2,000 or imprisonment not exceeding six months. By law, sexual intercourse with a female under 14 years of age constitutes rape and is punishable by imprisonment for not less than eight years and not more than 30 years and not less than 12 strokes of the cane. The intent of the law is to protect girls from exploitation through prostitution and "other immoral purposes", including pornography.[[155]](#cite_note-state.gov-158)

[Bruneian citizenship](/wiki/Bruneian_nationality_law "Bruneian nationality law") is derived through parents' nationality rather than *[jus soli](/wiki/Jus_soli "Jus soli")*. Parents with stateless status are required to apply for a special pass for a child born in the country. Failure to register a child may make it difficult to enroll the child in school.

#### Animal rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Animal rights")]

Brunei is the first country in [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") to have banned [shark finning](/wiki/Shark_finning "Shark finning") nationwide.[[156]](#cite_note-159)

Brunei has retained most of its forests, compared to its neighbours that share the island of [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"). There is a public campaign calling to protect [pangolins](/wiki/Pangolin "Pangolin") which are considered a threatened treasure in Brunei.[[157]](#cite_note-Hii2015-160)

#### LGBT rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: LGBT rights")]

Main article: [LGBT rights in Brunei](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Brunei "LGBT rights in Brunei")

Male and female homosexuality is [illegal in Brunei](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Brunei "LGBT rights in Brunei"). Sexual relations between men are punishable by death or whipping; sex between women is punishable by caning or imprisonment.

In May 2019, the Brunei government extended its existing moratorium on the death penalty to the Sharia criminal code, which had previously made homosexual acts punishable with death by stoning.[[158]](#cite_note-161)

In 2019, Brunei announced that it would no longer be implementing the second phase of its controversial sharia penal code. The code, which was first introduced in 2014, included a range of punishments for crimes such as theft, drug offences, and same-sex relationships, including amputation and death by stoning.

The decision to halt the implementation of the second phase of the code came after significant international backlash and pressure from countries and human rights organisations, who criticised the harsh punishments as inhumane and a violation of human rights.

The government of Brunei stated that the decision was made to maintain peace and stability in the country, and to avoid any negative impact on the economy and reputation of the country. The Sultan of Brunei, Hassanal Bolkiah, also issued a statement saying that the country would continue to "strengthen and improve" its legal system in line with international norms and best practices.

The first phase of the Sharia penal code, which includes fines and imprisonment for offences such as failure to attend Friday prayers and consuming alcohol, remains in place.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main articles: [Economy of Brunei](/wiki/Economy_of_Brunei "Economy of Brunei") and [Tourism in Brunei](/wiki/Tourism_in_Brunei "Tourism in Brunei")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/SBY_dan_para_pemimpin_ASEAN_25-04-2013.jpg/250px-SBY_dan_para_pemimpin_ASEAN_25-04-2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:SBY_dan_para_pemimpin_ASEAN_25-04-2013.jpg)

[BIMP-EAGA](/wiki/BIMP-EAGA "BIMP-EAGA") meeting in the office of Brunei Prime Minister on 25 April 2013. From left: Brunei Sultan Hassanal Bolkiah, Indonesian President [Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono](/wiki/Susilo_Bambang_Yudhoyono "Susilo Bambang Yudhoyono"), Malaysian Representative and Filipino President [Benigno Aquino III](/wiki/Benigno_Aquino_III "Benigno Aquino III"). Brunei is part of the BIMP-EAGA, a subregional economic co-operation initiative in Southeast Asia.

Brunei has the second-highest [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") among the [Southeast Asian](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") nations, after [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore").[[159]](#cite_note-162)[[160]](#cite_note-163) [Crude oil](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") and natural gas production account for about 90% of its GDP.[[102]](#cite_note-US_DOS-105) About 167,000 barrels (26,600 m3) of oil are produced every day, making Brunei the fourth-largest producer of oil in Southeast Asia.[[102]](#cite_note-US_DOS-105) It also produces approximately 25.3 million cubic metres (890 million cubic feet) of [liquified natural gas](/wiki/Liquified_natural_gas "Liquified natural gas") per day, making Brunei the ninth-largest gas exporter in the world.[[102]](#cite_note-US_DOS-105) *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* also ranks Brunei as the fifth-richest nation out of 182, based on its petroleum and natural gas fields.[[161]](#cite_note-164) Brunei was ranked 88th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[162]](#cite_note-165)[[163]](#cite_note-166)

Substantial income from overseas investment supplements income from domestic production. Most of these investments are made by the Brunei Investment Agency, an arm of the Ministry of Finance.[[102]](#cite_note-US_DOS-105) The government provides for all medical services,[[164]](#cite_note-167) and subsidises rice[[165]](#cite_note-168) and housing.[[102]](#cite_note-US_DOS-105)

The national [air carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier"), [Royal Brunei Airlines](/wiki/Royal_Brunei_Airlines "Royal Brunei Airlines"), is trying to develop Brunei as a hub for international travel between [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"). Central to this strategy is the position that the airline maintains at [London Heathrow Airport](/wiki/Heathrow_Airport "Heathrow Airport"). It holds a daily slot at the highly capacity-controlled airport, which it serves from [Bandar Seri Begawan](/wiki/Brunei_International_Airport "Brunei International Airport") via [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai_International_Airport "Dubai International Airport"). The airline also has services to other major [Asian](/wiki/Asia "Asia") destinations including [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong_International_Airport "Hong Kong International Airport"), [Bangkok](/wiki/Suvarnabhumi_Airport "Suvarnabhumi Airport"), [Singapore](/wiki/Changi_Airport "Changi Airport") and [Manila](/wiki/Ninoy_Aquino_International_Airport "Ninoy Aquino International Airport").

Brunei depends heavily on imports such as agricultural products (e.g. [rice](/wiki/Rice "Rice"), [food products](/wiki/Convenience_food "Convenience food"), [livestock](/wiki/Livestock "Livestock"), etc.),[[166]](#cite_note-agriculture_brunei-169) vehicles and [electrical products](/wiki/Consumer_electronics "Consumer electronics") from other countries.[[167]](#cite_note-170) Brunei imports 60% of its food; of that amount, around 75% come from other [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN") countries.[[166]](#cite_note-agriculture_brunei-169)

Brunei's leaders are concerned that increasing integration in the world economy will undermine internal [social cohesion](/wiki/Social_cohesion "Social cohesion") and have therefore pursued an isolationist policy. However, it has become a more prominent player by serving as chairman for the 2000 [Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation](/wiki/Asia-Pacific_Economic_Cooperation "Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation") (APEC) forum. Brunei's leaders plan to upgrade the [labour](/wiki/Labour_(economics) "Labour (economics)") force, reduce unemployment, which was at 6.9% in 2014;[[168]](#cite_note-171) strengthen the banking and tourism sectors, and, in general, broaden the economic base.[[169]](#cite_note-172) A long-term development plan aims to diversify growth.[[170]](#cite_note-173)

The government of Brunei has also promoted food self-sufficiency, especially in rice. Brunei renamed its Brunei Darussalam Rice 1 as Laila Rice during the launch of the "Padi Planting Towards Achieving Self-Sufficiency of Rice Production in Brunei Darussalam" ceremony at the Wasan padi fields in April 2009.[[171]](#cite_note-174) In August 2009, the Royal Family reaped the first few Laila padi stalks, after years of attempts to boost local rice production, a goal first articulated about half a century ago.[[172]](#cite_note-175) In July 2009 Brunei launched its national [halal](/wiki/Halal "Halal") branding scheme, Brunei Halal, with a goal to export to foreign markets.[[173]](#cite_note-176)

In 2020, Brunei's electricity production was largely based on fossil fuels; renewable energy accounted for less than 1% of produced electricity in the country.[[174]](#cite_note-177)

### Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/V8-DLA_Boeing_787_of_Royal_Brunei_at_Heathrow_%2811400528073%29.jpg/250px-V8-DLA_Boeing_787_of_Royal_Brunei_at_Heathrow_%2811400528073%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:V8-DLA_Boeing_787_of_Royal_Brunei_at_Heathrow_(11400528073).jpg)

Royal Brunei [Boeing 787 Dreamliner](/wiki/Boeing_787_Dreamliner "Boeing 787 Dreamliner") at London [Heathrow Airport](/wiki/Heathrow_Airport_Holdings "Heathrow Airport Holdings")
