As of 2019, the country's road network constituted a total length of 3,713.57 kilometres (2,307.51 mi), out of which 86.8% were [paved](/wiki/Paved_road "Paved road").[[175]](#cite_note-statistics_2019-178) The 135-kilometre (84 mi) highway from [Muara Town](/wiki/Muara_Town "Muara Town") to [Kuala Belait](/wiki/Kuala_Belait "Kuala Belait") is a [dual carriageway](/wiki/Dual_carriageway "Dual carriageway").[[127]](#cite_note-bruneipress.com.bn-130)

Brunei is accessible by air, sea, and land transport. [Brunei International Airport](/wiki/Brunei_International_Airport "Brunei International Airport") is the main entry point to the country. [Royal Brunei Airlines](/wiki/Royal_Brunei_Airlines "Royal Brunei Airlines")[[176]](#cite_note-179) is the national carrier. There is another airfield, the [Anduki Airfield](/wiki/Anduki_Airfield "Anduki Airfield"), located in [Seria](/wiki/Seria "Seria"). The ferry terminal at Muara services regular connections to [Labuan](/wiki/Labuan "Labuan") (Malaysia). Speedboats provide passenger and goods transportation to the Temburong district.[[177]](#cite_note-180) The main highway running across Brunei is the Tutong-Muara Highway. The country's road network is well developed. Brunei has one main sea port located at Muara.[[102]](#cite_note-US_DOS-105)

The airport in Brunei is currently being extensively upgraded.[[178]](#cite_note-181) Changi Airport International is the consultant working on this modernisation, which planned cost is currently $150 million.[[179]](#cite_note-BT_null-182)[[180]](#cite_note-Aviation-183) This project is slated to add 14,000 square metres (150,000 sq ft) of new floorspace and includes a new terminal and arrival hall.[[181]](#cite_note-BT22-184) With the completion of this project, the annual passenger capacity of the airport is expected to double from 1.5 to 3 million.[[179]](#cite_note-BT_null-182)

With one private car for every 2.09 persons, Brunei has one of the highest car ownership rates in the world. This has been attributed to the absence of a comprehensive transport system, low import tax, and low unleaded petrol price of B$0.53 per litre.[[127]](#cite_note-bruneipress.com.bn-130)

A new 30-kilometre (19 mi) roadway [connecting the Muara and Temburong](/wiki/Temburong_Bridge "Temburong Bridge") districts of opened to traffic on 17 March 2020.[[182]](#cite_note-185) One hundred and forty kilometres (87 miles) of this roadway would be crossing the [Brunei Bay](/wiki/Brunei_Bay "Brunei Bay").[[183]](#cite_note-186) The bridge cost is $1.6 billion.[[184]](#cite_note-187)

#### Banking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Banking")]

The [Bank of China](/wiki/Bank_of_China "Bank of China") received permission to open a branch in Brunei in April 2016. [Citibank](/wiki/Citibank "Citibank"), which entered in 1972, closed its operations in Brunei in 2014. [HSBC](/wiki/HSBC "HSBC"), which had entered in 1947, closed its operation in Brunei in November 2017.[[185]](#cite_note-HSBCClose-188) [Maybank of Malaysia](/wiki/Maybank "Maybank"), [RHB Bank of Malaysia](/wiki/RHB_Bank "RHB Bank"), [Standard Chartered Bank of United Kingdom](/wiki/Standard_Chartered "Standard Chartered"), [United Overseas Bank of Singapore](/wiki/United_Overseas_Bank "United Overseas Bank") and Bank of China are currently operating in Brunei.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main article: [Demographics of Brunei](/wiki/Demographics_of_Brunei "Demographics of Brunei")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Sultan_Omar_Ali_Saifuddin_Mosque_02.jpg/250px-Sultan_Omar_Ali_Saifuddin_Mosque_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sultan_Omar_Ali_Saifuddin_Mosque_02.jpg)

[Sultan Omar Ali Saifuddin Mosque](/wiki/Sultan_Omar_Ali_Saifuddin_Mosque "Sultan Omar Ali Saifuddin Mosque") at night

Ethnicities indigenous to Brunei include the [Belait](/wiki/Belait_people "Belait people"), [Brunei Bisaya](/wiki/Bisaya_(Borneo) "Bisaya (Borneo)") (not to be confused with the Bisaya/Visaya of the nearby Philippines), indigenous [Bruneian Malay](/wiki/Bruneian_Malay_people "Bruneian Malay people"), [Dusun](/wiki/Dusun_people "Dusun people"), [Kedayan](/wiki/Kedayan "Kedayan"), [Lun Bawang](/wiki/Lun_Bawang "Lun Bawang"), [Murut](/wiki/Murut_people "Murut people") and [Tutong](/wiki/Tutong_people "Tutong people").

The population of Brunei in 2021 was 445,373,[[186]](#cite_note-UN_WPP-189)[[187]](#cite_note-UN_WPP_2022-190) of which 76% live in urban areas. The rate of urbanisation is estimated at 2.13% per year from 2010 to 2015. The average life expectancy is 77.7 years.[[188]](#cite_note-191) In 2014, 65.7% of the population were [Malay](/wiki/Bruneian_Malay_people "Bruneian Malay people"), 10.3% are [Chinese](/wiki/Ethnic_Chinese_in_Brunei "Ethnic Chinese in Brunei"), 3.4% are indigenous, with 20.6% smaller groups making up the rest.[[189]](#cite_note-192) There is a relatively large [expatriate](/wiki/Expatriate "Expatriate") community.[[190]](#cite_note-193)
Most expats come from non-[Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") countries such as [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodia "Cambodia"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") and [India](/wiki/India "India").

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Religion")]

Main article: [Religion in Brunei](/wiki/Religion_in_Brunei "Religion in Brunei")

Religion in Brunei (2021)[[6]](#cite_note-deps.gov.bn-6)

1. [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") 362,035 (82.1%)
2. Christianity 29,462 (6.70%)
3. [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") 27,745 (6.30%)
4. Others: indigenous beliefs and non-religious, Atheist or Agnostic 21,473 (4.90%)

[Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Brunei "Islam in Brunei") is the official religion of Brunei,[[77]](#cite_note-cia-80) specifically that of the [Sunni](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") denomination and the [Shafi'i](/wiki/Shafi%27i "Shafi'i") school of Islamic jurisprudence. More than 82% of the population, including the majority of [Bruneian Malays](/wiki/Bruneian_Malay_people "Bruneian Malay people") and [Kedayans](/wiki/Kedayan "Kedayan"), identify as [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim"). Other faiths practised are [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Brunei "Christianity in Brunei") (6.7%) and [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Brunei "Buddhism in Brunei") (6.3%, mainly by the Chinese).[[6]](#cite_note-deps.gov.bn-6) [Freethinkers](/wiki/Freethought "Freethought"), mostly Chinese, form about 2% of the population. Although most of them practise some form of religion with elements of Buddhism, [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), and [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"), they prefer to present themselves as having practised no religion officially, hence labelled as [atheists](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism") in official censuses.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Followers of indigenous religions are about 2% of the population.[[191]](#cite_note-194)

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Language")]

Main article: [Languages of Brunei](/wiki/Languages_of_Brunei "Languages of Brunei")

The official [language of Brunei](/wiki/Languages_of_Brunei "Languages of Brunei") is [Standard Malay](/wiki/Standard_Malay "Standard Malay"), for which both the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") ([Rumi](/wiki/Rumi_script "Rumi script")) and the [Arabic alphabet](/wiki/Arabic_alphabet "Arabic alphabet") ([Jawi](/wiki/Jawi_alphabet "Jawi alphabet")) are used.[[192]](#cite_note-195) Initially, Malay was written in the [Jawi script](/wiki/Jawi_script "Jawi script") before it switched to the Latin alphabet around 1941.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEde_Vienne2016104-196)

The principal spoken language is *Melayu Brunei* ([Brunei Malay](/wiki/Brunei_Malay "Brunei Malay")). Brunei Malay is rather divergent from standard Malay and the rest of the Malay dialects, being about 84% cognate with standard Malay,[[194]](#cite_note-Martin-197) and is mostly mutually intelligible with it.[[195]](#cite_note-198)

English is widely used as a business and official language and it is spoken by a majority of the population in Brunei. English is used in business as a [working language](/wiki/Working_language "Working language") and as the language of instruction from primary to tertiary education.[[196]](#cite_note-199)[[197]](#cite_note-200)[[198]](#cite_note-201)[[199]](#cite_note-202)

Chinese languages are also widely spoken, and the [Chinese minority](/wiki/Ethnic_Chinese_in_Brunei "Ethnic Chinese in Brunei") in Brunei speaks a number of [varieties of Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese").

[Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") is the religious language of Muslims and is taught in schools, particularly religious schools, and also in institutes of higher learning. As of 2004, there are six Arabic schools and one religious teachers' college in Brunei. A majority of Brunei's Muslim population has had some form of formal or informal education in the reading, writing and pronunciation of the Arabic language as part of their religious education.

Other languages and dialects spoken include [Kedayan Malay](/wiki/Kedayan_Malay "Kedayan Malay") dialect, [Tutong Malay dialect](/wiki/Tutong_language "Tutong language"), [Murut](/wiki/Murutic_languages "Murutic languages"), and [Dusun](/wiki/Dusun_language "Dusun language").[[194]](#cite_note-Martin-197)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Culture")]

Main article: [Culture of Brunei](/wiki/Culture_of_Brunei "Culture of Brunei")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Royal_Regalie_%281%29.JPG/250px-Royal_Regalie_%281%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Royal_Regalie_(1).JPG)

Royal Regalia Museum

The culture of Brunei is predominantly [Malay](/wiki/Malay_people "Malay people") (reflecting its ethnicity), with heavy influences from [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), but is seen as much more conservative than [Indonesia](/wiki/Culture_of_Indonesia "Culture of Indonesia") and [Malaysia](/wiki/Culture_of_Malaysia "Culture of Malaysia").[[200]](#cite_note-203) Influences to Bruneian culture come from the Malay cultures of the [Malay Archipelago](/wiki/Malay_Archipelago "Malay Archipelago"). Four periods of cultural influence have occurred: [animist](/wiki/Animism "Animism"), [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu"), Islamic, and [Western](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture"). Islam had a very strong influence, and was adopted as Brunei's ideology and philosophy.[[201]](#cite_note-204)

As a [Sharia](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") country, the sale and public consumption of alcohol is banned.[[202]](#cite_note-Brunei_Tourism_Website-205) Non-Muslims are allowed to bring in a limited amount of alcohol from their point of embarkation overseas for their own private consumption.[[127]](#cite_note-bruneipress.com.bn-130)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [Media of Brunei](/wiki/Media_of_Brunei "Media of Brunei")

Media in Brunei are said to be pro-government; press criticism of the government and monarchy is rare. The country ranks "Not Free" in media by [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House").[[203]](#cite_note-206) Nonetheless, the press is not overtly hostile toward alternative viewpoints and is not restricted to publishing only articles regarding the government. The government allowed a printing and publishing company, Brunei Press PLC, to form in 1953. The company continues to print the English daily *[Borneo Bulletin](/wiki/Borneo_Bulletin "Borneo Bulletin")*. This paper began as a weekly [community paper](/wiki/Community_paper "Community paper") and became a daily in 1990[[127]](#cite_note-bruneipress.com.bn-130) Apart from The *Borneo Bulletin*, there is also the *[Media Permata](/wiki/Media_Permata "Media Permata")* and Pelita Brunei, the local Malay newspapers which are circulated daily. *[The Brunei Times](/wiki/The_Brunei_Times "The Brunei Times")* is another English independent newspaper published in Brunei since 2006.[[204]](#cite_note-207)

The Brunei government, through state broadcaster [Radio Television Brunei](/wiki/Radio_Television_Brunei "Radio Television Brunei") (RTB), owns and operates three television channels with the introduction of digital TV using DVB-T (RTB Perdana, RTB Aneka and RTB Sukmaindera) and five radio stations (National FM, Pilihan FM, Nur Islam FM, Harmony FM and Pelangi FM). A private company has made cable television available (Astro-Kristal) as well as one private radio station, Kristal FM.[[127]](#cite_note-bruneipress.com.bn-130)
It also has an online campus radio station, *[UBD FM](/wiki/UBD_FM "UBD FM")*, that streams from its first university, [Universiti Brunei Darussalam](/wiki/Universiti_Brunei_Darussalam "Universiti Brunei Darussalam").[[205]](#cite_note-208)

### Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brunei&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Sport")]

Main article: [Sport in Brunei](/wiki/Sport_in_Brunei "Sport in Brunei")

The most popular sport in Brunei is [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"). The [Brunei national football team](/wiki/Brunei_national_football_team "Brunei national football team") joined [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") in 1969, but has not had much success. Brunei's top football league is the [Brunei Super League](/wiki/Brunei_Super_League "Brunei Super League"), which is managed by the [Football Association of Brunei Darussalam](/wiki/Football_Association_of_Brunei_Darussalam "Football Association of Brunei Darussalam") (FABD). The nation has its own [martial arts](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts") called "Silat Suffian Bela Diri".[[206]](#cite_note-209)

Brunei [debuted at the Olympics](/wiki/Brunei_at_the_Olympics "Brunei at the Olympics") in 1996 and has competed at all subsequent [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympics "Summer Olympics") except the 2008 edition. The country has competed in badminton, shooting, swimming, and track-and-field, but has yet to win any medals. The [Brunei Darussalam National Olympic Council](/wiki/Brunei_Darussalam_National_Olympic_Council "Brunei Darussalam National Olympic Council") is the [National Olympic Committee](/wiki/National_Olympic_Committee "National Olympic Committee") for Brunei.

Brunei has had slightly more success [at the Asian Games](/wiki/Brunei_at_the_Asian_Games "Brunei at the Asian Games"), winning four bronze medals. The first major international sporting event to be hosted in Brunei was the [1999 Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/1999_Southeast_Asian_Games "1999 Southeast Asian Games"). According to the [all-time Southeast Asian Games medal table](/wiki/All-time_Southeast_Asian_Games_medal_table "All-time Southeast Asian Games medal table"), Bruneian athletes have won a total of 14 gold, 55 silver and 163 bronze medals at the games.
